en.keyword.meta.boolean_expression_simplifi: Simplify any boolean algebra expression instantly. Supports SOP notation, De Morgan law reduction, truth table generation and gate diagram output.
en.keyword.meta.truth_table_to_logic_circui: Convert truth-table output patterns into simplified Boolean expressions and practical gate-level circuit plans for faster validation.

keyword.inline.copy.heroTagline: Use this tool directly — no redirects, no sign-up required.

truth.inputHint: Click the Output cells to toggle between 0 and 1.

boolean.errorParse: Could not parse expression. Check syntax: use A, B, C for variables and +, *, A' for operators.

howItWorks.title: How Boolean Expression Simplification Works
howItWorks.intro: Boolean algebra simplification reduces complex logical expressions into their minimal equivalent form using a set of algebraic laws. Every digital circuit, from basic gates to complex processors, operates on boolean logic — and simplification directly reduces gate count, power consumption and propagation delay in real hardware.

cards.title: Sum of Products (SOP)
cards.description: The standard form where AND terms are ORed together. Every boolean function can be expressed in SOP and it maps directly to a two-level AND-OR gate network.

cards.title: Prime Implicants
cards.description: A prime implicant is a product term that cannot be combined with another term to produce a simpler term. The Quine-McCluskey method finds all prime implicants systematically.

cards.title: Essential Prime Implicants
cards.description: An essential prime implicant covers at least one minterm that no other prime implicant covers. The minimum cover must include all essential prime implicants.

identities.title: De Morgan's Laws and Boolean Identities
identities.intro: These fundamental identities allow you to transform and simplify boolean expressions. The simplifier applies these automatically.

complement.name: Complement
complement.description: A variable OR its complement is always 1

idempotent.name: Idempotent
idempotent.description: Repeated terms collapse to a single term

quine.title: Quine-McCluskey Algorithm Step by Step
quine.intro: The Quine-McCluskey method is a tabular technique that systematically finds the minimum SOP form of a boolean function. Unlike K-maps it works for any number of variables and is computer-friendly.

steps.title: List all minterms.
steps.description: Convert each row where the output is 1 to its binary representation and group by number of 1-bits.

steps.title: Merge adjacent groups.
steps.description: Combine pairs that differ in exactly one bit position, replacing that bit with a dash (don't-care). Repeat until no more merges are possible.

steps.title: Identify prime implicants.
steps.description: Any term that could not be merged further is a prime implicant. Collect all of them into a prime implicant chart.

steps.title: Select essential prime implicants.
steps.description: Find every minterm covered by exactly one prime implicant — that implicant is essential. Include all essentials in the final expression.

steps.title: Cover remaining minterms.
steps.description: For any minterms not yet covered by essentials, choose additional prime implicants greedily to minimize the total term count.

keyword.boolean.faq.question: What notation does the Boolean Expression Simplifier accept?
keyword.boolean.faq.answer: The tool accepts multiple notations simultaneously. You can use '+' for OR, '·' or '*' or just adjacency for AND, and apostrophe (A') or '!' for NOT. Parentheses for grouping are fully supported.

keyword.boolean.faq.question: What simplification algorithm is used?
keyword.boolean.faq.answer: The tool uses the Quine-McCluskey algorithm, which is the provably optimal method for finding prime implicants and selecting essential ones. It guarantees the minimum SOP form.

keyword.boolean.faq.question: How many variables are supported?
keyword.boolean.faq.answer: Up to 5 variables (A through E) are supported, producing up to 32-row truth tables. For most practical digital logic problems this is sufficient.

keyword.boolean.faq.question: What is the output format of the simplified expression?
keyword.boolean.faq.answer: The result is shown in standard Sum-of-Products (SOP) form using the apostrophe notation (e.g. A'B + AB'). The truth table and a gate network diagram are also generated automatically.

keyword.boolean.faq.question: Can I use this for Karnaugh map problems?
keyword.boolean.faq.answer: Yes. The Quine-McCluskey algorithm is equivalent to solving a K-map but works for more than 4 variables where K-maps are impractical.

keyword.boolean.faq.question: Does the tool handle expressions that are always true or always false?
keyword.boolean.faq.answer: Yes. Tautologies (always 1) and contradictions (always 0) are detected automatically and displayed as constant outputs.

keyword.truth.content.overview: This workflow converts output-bit behavior into a simplified Boolean expression and a practical gate-stage plan for quick implementation.

sections.title: Boolean Reduction Pipeline
sections.description: The tool validates variable and bit lengths, extracts minterms, and reduces the expression so the final equation is easier to implement.

diagramSteps.label: Validate
diagramSteps.detail: Variable count determines required truth-table rows before any reduction starts.

diagramSteps.label: Extract
diagramSteps.detail: Rows with output 1 are converted into minterm indexes.

diagramSteps.label: Reduce
diagramSteps.detail: Compatible terms are merged to produce a shorter Boolean expression.

sections.tableCaption: Core checks used for expression minimization

sections.title: Gate-Level Planning and Verification
sections.description: After simplification, the tool estimates gate stages and provides a compact implementation path for NOT, AND, and OR composition.

diagramSteps.label: Invert
diagramSteps.detail: Only required complemented literals are sent through NOT stages.

diagramSteps.label: Combine
diagramSteps.detail: Product terms are created with AND gates based on reduced expression terms.

diagramSteps.label: Merge
diagramSteps.detail: Final OR stage collects term outputs into the target function.

faqItems.question: How many variables are supported on this page?
faqItems.answer: The inline tool accepts between 1 and 4 variables.

faqItems.question: Why must output bits match exactly 2^n rows?
faqItems.answer: Each row represents one input combination. Missing or extra bits break row alignment.

faqItems.question: Is this useful for class assignments and prototyping?
faqItems.answer: Yes. It is useful for quick derivation, checking simplification, and preparing gate-level drafts.
